methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $252.6B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
ARC Clean Technology, a Series G nuclear energy company founded by Don Wolf, has raised over $50M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a comparable universe of eight private and three public nuclear energy firms, including peers such as Oklo, TerraPowers, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy.

The stage-progression method is the primary applicable valuation approach. Using total capital raised as a proxy base of $252.6B and applying a 1.25x step-up multiplier typical for Series G advancement yields an implied valuation of $315.8B. The peer-median method was not applied due to insufficient narrow comparables; while eight same-sector peers exist, none satisfied minimum data requirements (fewer than three datapoints met same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier filters), making sector-wide median estimation unreliable and potentially misleading.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $268.4B (low) to $363.1B (high), with a median point of $315.8B. Critical uncertainties include the validity of total raised as a valuation proxy—stage-progression reliance on a single method introduces methodological concentration risk. The narrow nuclear energy peer set, while relevant, limits confidence in cross-validation. Capital efficiency, path to revenue, and regulatory risk remain unquantified inputs to downside scenarios. A more robust range would require either expansion of applicable comparable data or introduction of cash flow-based approaches, neither of which the current dataset supports.
